title: Multi-Modal Spectroscopy Theory for Ultrafast Control of Rabi Oscillations
authors: J. W. Yu, H. B Wang, X. Q. Zhou, M. Tang, Z. B. Ni, X. T. Cheng, Y. Zhao, S. N Ding, J. Y. Yan, H. H. Zhu, C. H. Li, F. Liu, C. Y. Jin
abstract: We propose a three-cavity scheme to realize full control of the emitter-cavity coupling strength in cavity quantum electrodynamics (cQED). The involvement of coupled oscillators gives rise to transient dynamics comprising multiple spectral components, which significantly increases the numerical cost to resolve the fluorescence spectrum in the time domain. A generalized sensor method is hence developed to simplify the calculation process for the characterization of nonstationary quantum dynamics. Multi-modal spectroscopy reveals the emergence, splitting, and disappearance of supermodes in real time. Based on the depletion of the zero-energy supermode, ultrafast switching of Rabi oscillations is demonstrated for time-domain multi-modal spectroscopy. These results exhibit a consistent picture from the spectral control of multiple oscillators to the quantum observation in ultrafast dynamics, which establishes the sensor method as a powerful theoretical tool for the ultrafast spectroscopy of cQED systems.
summary: This paper introduces a new theoretical method to efficiently simulate the transient spectra of complex, time-dependent cavity QED systems. By using auxiliary 'sensor' modes, the authors bypass the high computational cost of traditional methods, allowing them to demonstrate how to switch Rabi oscillations on and off at ultrafast speeds by manipulating cavity detuning.
main_problem: Achieving efficient, real-time control and characterization of light-matter interactions in multi-mode, nonstationary cavity QED systems, while overcoming the computational bottleneck of resolving transient spectra in the time domain.
main_result: The development of a generalized sensor method that enables efficient calculation of time-resolved physical spectra (TRPS) and demonstrates the ultrafast switching of Rabi oscillations via supermode redistribution.
method: A generalized sensor method using weakly coupled auxiliary modes as frequency-selective probes to compute TRPS with linear scaling, alongside numerical simulations using the Lindblad master equation.
model_or_system: A three-cavity scheme consisting of a two-level system (TLS) embedded in a middle cavity, coupled to two lateral cavities, described by a time-dependent Hamiltonian in the single-excitation subspace.
key_observables: TLS excited state population, cavity mode populations, and Time-Resolved Physical Spectrum (TRPS).
important_parameters: Coupling strengths (g, eta), detuning (Delta), dissipation rates (gamma, kappa), and the ratio g/eta.
main_assumptions: The system operates in the strong coupling regime; the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA) is applied; the sensor coupling (epsilon) is kept weak to avoid perturbing intrinsic dynamics.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of the TLS-three-cavity system and supermode localization. Fig 2: Supermode eigenfrequencies vs detuning. Fig 5: Runtime scaling comparison of the sensor method vs analytical method. Fig 6: Validation of the sensor method. Fig 9-10: TRPS and population dynamics showing Rabi oscillation control and switching effects. Fig 11-12: Robustness analysis regarding detuning amplitude and switching time.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the three-cavably cQED problem, defines the Hamiltonian and supermode structure, proposes the sensor method for efficient TRPS calculation, validates the method against analytical benchmarks, and demonstrates the physical application of ultrafast Rabi oscillation control via detuning-controlled supermode redistribution.
why_interesting: It provides a computationally efficient framework for studying non-equilibrium dynamics in multi-mode systems and demonstrates a practical mechanism for ultrafast control of light-matter coupling, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the TLS-three-cavity sys- tem. A two-level system (TLS) is embedded in the middle cavity and couples to its local photonic mode with strength g. The two lateral cavities are coupled to the middle cavity with strength η, and their resonance frequencies are tuned to ω0 + ∆and ω0 −∆, respectively. The supermode of the three-cavity system at ω0 is mainly distributed over the lateral cavities and nearly vanishes in the middle cavity when ∆= 0 (yellow shaded region). In contrast, when |∆| ≫η, the ω0 mode becomes localized in the middle cavity (black dashed outline). This detuning-controlled redistribution of the op- tical density of states (DOS) in the middle...

FIG. 2. (a) Eigenfrequencies of the three supermodes as functions of the normalized detuning ∆/η. The central super- mode remains pinned at ω0 and is independent of ∆, whereas the upper and lower supermodes split symmetrically with in- creasing |∆|. (b) Effective coupling strengths between the TLS and the three supermodes. In particular, the TLS- central-supermode coupling strength geff can be tuned con- tinuously from −g to g by varying ∆, offering a significantly large tuning range.

FIG. 3. Numerical result for the Rabi oscillation of the occupation of the excited state of TLS and population of the three modes. The system is initially at the excited state of the TLS. In (a), the detuning is not changing during the entire process. In (b) and (c) the detuning is turned from 1.2η to 0 at the vertical dashed line, while in (d) the detuning is turned from 0 to 1.2η. The other parameters including coupling constants and dissipation rates are given in the text.

FIG. 4. Schematic illustration of a time-resolved physical spectrum (TRPS) measurement. The emitted field is passed through a Lorentzian spectral filter, and the time-dependent intensity of the filtered signal is recorded by a sensor, yielding a frequency-resolved temporal profile of the emission.

FIG. 6. Validation of the sensor method under continuously time-dependent modulation. (a) Smooth switching of the de- tuning with a finite fall time of 3 ps (dashed line), together with the population dynamics of the TLS and cavity modes. (b)-(c) Comparison of the TRPS obtained from the analytical definition in Eq. (8) and the sensor method.
